steps.title: The Three Laws
steps.text:
Every great opening — from the Italian to the Sicilian — rests on the same three pillars. Master them and you'll never have a *bad* opening, even if you've never seen the position before.

## The three laws of the opening

1. **Control the center.** The four squares **d4, e4, d5, e5** are the most valuable real estate on the board. Pieces that control them reach more squares; pieces that don't are spectators.
2. **Develop your pieces quickly.** Get every minor piece (knights, bishops) off the back rank in the first 8–10 moves. *Don't* move the same piece twice unless you must.
3. **Get your king safe.** Castle by move 10. A king in the middle is a king in danger.

A model opening sequence: {{e4}} {{e5}} {{Nf3}} {{Nc6}} {{Bc4}} {{Bc5}} {{O-O}}. White has already done all three things — pawn in the center, two pieces developed, king tucked away.

> Develop with purpose. Castle without delay. The middlegame begins where the opening principles end.

steps.prompt: Stake your claim with a central pawn — play **{{e4}}** or **{{d4}}**.
steps.hint: Both moves grab a center square and open lines for two pieces at once (queen and a bishop). That's the cheapest first-move principle.

steps.title: Now Develop
steps.text:
The pawns lay the foundation; now the pieces move in.

## Order of development

- **Pawns first** — but only the central ones, just enough to open lines.
- **Knights before bishops.** Knights have fewer good squares (usually f3, c3 or f6, c6), so develop them first to commit fewer choices.
- **Bishops to active squares.** They want diagonals that hit the enemy camp.
- **Queen last among the minor pieces.** She's too valuable to expose early.
- **Rooks last of all** — they finish the development by occupying open files.

Why knights first? Because **{{Nf3}}** does triple duty: develops a piece, attacks the e5 pawn, and prepares castling. Few moves accomplish so much at once.

> Move every piece once before you move any piece twice.

steps.prompt: Develop a knight toward the center — **{{Nf3}}** is the classical choice (attacks e5), or **{{Nc3}}** also works.
steps.hint: Both moves bring a knight into the action. Which one also attacks Black's e5 pawn?

steps.title: Bishops Next
steps.text:
Both knights belong on f3 and c3 (or c4 in some openings). Now the bishops want to be unleashed.

## Choosing a bishop's home

- **{{Bc4}}** — the *Italian Game* setup. The bishop eyes f7, the most vulnerable square in Black's camp because only the king defends it.
- **{{Bb5}}** — the *Ruy Lopez* setup. Pressures the c6 knight (which defends e5) — chess's oldest serious opening.
- **{{Bd3}}** — solid but blocks the d-pawn. Common in 1.d4 systems.
- **{{Be2}}** — modest but flexible. The bishop supports a kingside attack later.

Notice that Black has mirrored you with {{Nc6}}. Symmetry is fine for a few moves — the player who breaks symmetry usefully often gets the advantage.

> A bishop with a target is worth two without one.

steps.prompt: Develop your light-squared bishop. **{{Bc4}}** is the classical Italian — it stares at f7.
steps.hint: Where does Black's f7 pawn need defending? A bishop on c4 strikes that exact square.

steps.title: King Safety
steps.text:
Two minor pieces out, center claimed, lines open — this is the ideal moment to **castle**.

Castling does three things in one move:

- Tucks the king into a corner, behind a wall of pawns
- Connects the rooks (they can now defend each other along the back rank)
- Brings a rook to the center — usually f1 or d1, where it controls a half-open file

## A useful checklist before castling

- Is the **f-pawn** still on its starting square? (Moving it weakens the castled king.)
- Are there any **immediate threats** I should meet first?
- Will I castle into the **same side** as my opponent (positional game) or the **opposite side** (sharp pawn-storm fight)?

From the position above, the natural follow-up is {{O-O}}. After that, you'll think about the d-file, the c-pawn break, and active rook moves.

> Castle early, castle on time, and the middlegame opens with you in shape.
